one_line_takeaway: A capstone benchmark showing FGSM and CW collapse three pretrained CNNs to >90% Top-1 error at small perturbations, that CW costs ~10x more compute than FGSM, and that defensive distillation rescues accuracy against FGSM (0.55->0.87) but not against CW.
key_finding: On Tiny ImageNet both attacks drive all three models to catastrophic error at small epsilon, and the two attacks reach essentially identical peak error rates -- but CW is roughly an order of magnitude slower per iteration than FGSM (e.g. ResNeXt50: 2.35s vs 24.9s average per iteration over 157 iterations). For defense: defensive distillation restored ResNeXt50 accuracy under FGSM on CIFAR-10 (clean 0.79, post-FGSM 0.55, post-distillation 0.87) but provided no improvement against CW, reproducing the well-known result that distillation fails against optimization-based attacks.
core_contribution: An empirical evaluation (a Praxis Tech School capstone report) that compares two evasion attacks -- FGSM and the L2 Carlini-Wagner (CW) attack -- against three ImageNet-pretrained CNN classifiers (ResNeXt50_32x4d, DenseNet201, VGG19) on Tiny ImageNet, and then tests defensive distillation as a defense on CIFAR-10. It is a comparison/benchmarking study rather than a new attack or defense; the paper's own contribution is the head-to-head measurement of attack potency and cost, plus the finding that a teacher(ResNet101)->student(ResNeXt50) distillation setup helps against FGSM but not against CW.
threat_model: Inference-time evasion of image classifiers via adversarial examples. The attacker perturbs input pixels by a bounded magnitude epsilon (swept 1%-10% of the 0-255 range) to induce misclassification. Both attacks are white-box, gradient-based: FGSM is a one-step sign-of-gradient perturbation; CW is an iterative optimization (Lp-norm-constrained) that seeks the minimal high-confidence adversarial perturbation. The defender's goal is to preserve classification accuracy under these perturbations, evaluated here via defensive distillation (training a student on temperature-softened teacher logits).
limitations: The reported FGSM and CW error tables (Tables 4-6 vs 8-10) are near-identical across every epsilon value, which the authors themselves flag as surprising ('test errors are mostly the same in both cases'); this strongly suggests a measurement/implementation artifact and undercuts the attack comparison. A second data quirk: the post-distillation accuracy (0.87) exceeds the clean accuracy (0.79), which is left unexplained. No external baselines or SOTA defenses (adversarial training, etc.) are actually run -- comparison is only self-referential. Defensive distillation is tested on only one dataset (CIFAR-10) with a single student architecture and only reports accuracy at unspecified aggregate epsilon. MNIST, CIFAR-100, and full ImageNet are described but never used in experiments. As a student capstone it lacks statistical repetition and adaptive-attack evaluation.

datasets_used: Tiny ImageNet (200 classes) -- clean baselines and FGSM/CW attack evaluation on the pretrained models
CIFAR-10 (60,000 32x32 images, 10 classes; 40k train / 10k val / 10k test split used) -- defensive distillation experiments
ImageNet -- the three CNNs were pretrained on it (via torchvision) before evaluation
quantitative_results: Clean Top-1 error (Tiny ImageNet): ResNeXt50_32x4d 10.16%, DenseNet201 13.92%, VGG19 19.88%
Clean Top-5 error: ResNeXt50_32x4d 1.20%, DenseNet201 2.22%, VGG19 4.38%
Peak FGSM error: ResNeXt50 Top-1 91.80% / Top-5 61.66%; DenseNet201 94.66% / 67.94%; VGG19 98.10% / 80.84%
Peak CW error: essentially identical to FGSM -- ResNeXt50 91.80% / 61.66%; DenseNet201 94.66% / 67.94%; VGG19 98.10% / 80.84%
Attack cost (157 iterations each): FGSM avg iteration time 2.35s / 2.21s / 1.65s vs CW 24.9s / 27.4s / 34.7s for ResNeXt50 / DenseNet201 / VGG19
Defensive distillation on CIFAR-10 (ResNeXt50 student): clean accuracy 0.79, post-FGSM 0.55, post-distillation 0.87; against CW distillation gave no accuracy improvement
baselines_compared: FGSM vs CW (L2) attack -- head-to-head on potency and compute cost
Three CNN architectures against each other: ResNeXt50_32x4d, DenseNet201, VGG19
With vs without defensive distillation (ResNet101 teacher -> ResNeXt50_32x4d student) under FGSM and under CW
